coredata.dc:title: Tunable upconversion emission from oil-based carbon nanodots
coredata.dc:creator: Kumar Reddy Bogireddy, Naveen
Agarwal, Vivechana
coredata.dc:description: Optical properties of carbon dots (CDs), prepared using 10-Undeceonic acid as an organic precursor, exhibited broad range excitation wavelength-dependent photoluminescence, with maximum emission intensity at 3.5 eV. Besides conventional down-conversion PL (DCPL), CDs show excitation wavelength-dependent upconversion PL(UCPL), produced by electromagnetic radiation in the green-NIR wavelength range. The difference between the excitation energy and the UCPL peak energy is found to be constant upon varying the excitation energy, whereas the energy difference with respect to DCPL peak energy decreases linearly. UCPL/DCPL have been explained with the help of surface states, shallow and deep trap states present in the proposed CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 10-Undeceonic acid
Carbon dots
Upconversion
Two-photon
PL

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photoluminescence (PL) excited spectra at (A) 500 nm, (B) 550 nm, (C) 600 nm and (D) 650 nm (NPL-Normal fluorescence, and UCPL-Upconversion fluorescence).

(A) UCPL spectra of CDs at 900 sec (Sample 4) reaction time and (B) plot represents the energy difference between excitation and emission energy (for both down (normal) and Upconversion photoluminescence) from Figs. S2 and S4; X axis represents changes in excitation wavelength.

PLExcitation of CDs as a function of time (A) S2, (B) S3, (C) S4 and (D) Simplified energy diagram for upconversion PL. CB: conduction band; VB: valance band; SS: surface state; STS: shallow trap state; DTS: deep trap state; TP: two-photon process; VS: virtual state, excitation wavelength dependent DCPL emission (Figs. S2, S3); λex: excitation wavelength; λem: emission wavelength.

head.ce:title: Tunable upconversion emission from oil-based carbon nanodots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centro de Investigación en Ingeniería y Ciencias Aplicadas, UAEM, Av. Univ. 1001, Col. Chamilpa, Cuernavaca, Morelos 62209, Mexico

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centro de Investigación en Ingeniería y Ciencias Aplicadas

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centro de Investigación en Ingeniería y Ciencias Aplicadas, UAEM, Av. Univ. 1001, Col. Chamilpa, Cuernavaca, Morelos 62209, Mexico

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: (Present address) Instituto de Física, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México (UNAM), Ciudad de México C.P. 04510, Mexico
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: (Present address) Instituto de Física, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México (UNAM), Ciudad de México C.P. 04510, Mexico

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Optical properties of carbon dots (CDs), prepared using 10-Undeceonic acid as an organic precursor, exhibited broad range excitation wavelength-dependent photoluminescence, with maximum emission intensity at 3.5 eV. Besides conventional down-conversion PL (DCPL), CDs show excitation wavelength-dependent upconversion PL(UCPL), produced by electromagnetic radiation in the green-NIR wavelength range. The difference between the excitation energy and the UCPL peak energy is found to be constant upon varying the excitation energy, whereas the energy difference with respect to DCPL peak energy decreases linearly. UCPL/DCPL have been explained with the help of surface states, shallow and deep trap states present in the proposed CDs.

ce:section.ce:para: During the past decade, CDs have drawn considerable attention in the fields of biomedicine, drug delivery, optoelectronic devices, and biomarkers [1,2]. CDs are promising alternatives due to their chemical stability, solubility, low toxicity, biodegradability, optical properties, high resistance to photobleaching [2,3]. From their strong wavelength-dependent and independent photoluminescence (PL), facile synthesized CDs have broad applications in photo-catalysis, bioimaging, and energy storage. Moreover, CDs possess excellent PL properties, allowing the design of complex and high-performance catalytic systems. CDs emit NIR under NIR excitation [4]. They are helpful for in-vivo bio-nanotechnology applications (i.e., water windows). Although most applications have been focused on PL-related properties, upconversion PL(UCPL) in CDs opens new prospects for highly efficient cell imaging with two-photon PL microscopy [5].

On the other hand, UC PL has been commonly observed in heterostructures of quantum dots (QDs) [17], triplet–triplet annihilation [18], and lanthanide-doped UC nanoparticles [19]. These studies considered intermediate states to be a criterion for UCPL, which could provide a transition level to absorb carriers excited by the first photon with subsequent transfer to higher energy states. The typical example was UC NPs, where low-energy photons (infrared light) could be effectively converted into ultraviolet or visible photons due to their ladder-like energy levels [6]. The preparation of CDs with alteration in synthesis shows different PL colors, indicating the possible bandgap engineering. Inspired by these excellent PL properties, CDs have attained a boom in terms of the synthesis routes and the detailed study of their optical properties. Since their discovery in 2004, till date, various synthetic procedures have been developed for the preparation of CDs, namely microwave synthesis [7], thermal decomposition of precursors (e.g., citric acid [8], glucose [9], etc.), electrochemical oxidation [10] and combustion of candle soot [11].

Additionally, the preparation of CDs with alteration in synthesis parameters has been shown to influence the PL, as mentioned earlier, indicating the possible route for bandgap engineering. This work introduces an organic precursor to synthesize CDs, using a simple one-step bottom-up method by carbonizing 10-undecenoic acid (10-UA) to obtain both conventional PL and UCPL. In a single excitation without using any inorganic or organic additives for surface passivation makes it attractive compared to other reported methods using surface passivation [12].

The pyrolysis was carried out in open air, as a function of time (samples namely: S1 to S5), i.e., 30, 160, 480, 900, and 1200 sec at 280 ± 5 0C, using a heating mantle. Measured absorbance spectra (Fig. S1) of CDs as a function of pyrolysis time show two deep UV absorbance peaks at 227 ± 3 nm and 282 ± 2 nm for all the samples. From PL studies (Fig. S2) the observed energy difference between excitation and emission wavelengths is approx. 0.76, 0.87, 0.91, 0.93, and 1.0 eV. Interestingly, increased broadness and a redshift is observed with increasing pyrolysis heating time. The two distinct absorption peaks have been attributed to π → π* and δ → π* transitions. The absorption tail is observed as the surface states related to the hybrid structure between the CD core and related chemical groups [13].

In particular, the S2-CDs showed (Fig. 1 a) existence of both green and red emission centered at ∼ 533 (∼2.3 eV) and ∼ 614 nm (2.01 eV) excited by 500 nm. Further, it is interesting to note that CDs possessed both standard and upconversion PL under the excitation range 550 nm ≤ λex ≤ 650 nm (Fig. 1(b-d)). In addition, the energy differences between excitation wavelengths (550, 600, and 650 nm) and UCPL emission is very similar, i.e.,∼ 1.29 (Fig. 1b), ∼1.34 (Fig. 1c), and ∼ 1.32 eV (Fig. 1d). Interestingly, when excited with 550 nm, the CDs exhibited a solid red emission centered at 620 nm (2 eV) with an observable UCPL emission at 350 nm.

To observe the upconversion PL, when CDs are excited from 600 to 1000 nm with 50 nm step, the UCPL emission shifts from 445 to 550 nm (Fig. 2a) (Figs. S2, S3). UCPL intensity increased with increased excitation wavelength from 700 to 850 nm (Figs. S4, S5), and the intensity decreased with increasing excitation wavelength from 850 to 1000 nm. As per the existing reports [17–19], the carbine ground-state is related to energy differences (δE) between the excitation and upconversion emission energy; δE should be below 1.5 eV for a triplet ground state (as per Hoffmann determined ΔE < 1.5 eV). In the present case, the δEs (UCPL) for 3 different CDs i.e., samples S2, S3 and S4 (ref to Figs. S3, S4 and S5) are approx. 1.1, 1.03, and 1.03eV, respectively (upper section Fig. 2b) are within the required range mentioned above. All obtained CDs exhibited excitation wavelength-dependent UCPL behaviors [14,15]. But in standard PL, ΔE decreased from high to low excitation wavelengths for all samples (Fig. 2b) (Figs. S4, S5) [16]. The hydrodynamic size of fabricated CDs are presented in Table S1.

From the FTIR (Fig. S6) analysis (supporting data), apart from the observed functional groups, the surface states with various energy levels, could be correlated to π to π* and n to π* transition states (measured through absorbtion spectra; Fig. S1) corresponding to the conjugated C = C, and C = O respectively. Possibly, due to the difference in the chemical bonding between different functional groups, a series of emissive traps are revealed from PLEx spectra of CDs (Fig. 3). The efficient edge functional groups for green and blue emission is attributed to carboxyl and hydroxyl groups respectively [7]. Moreover, there are 2 possible mechanisms (i.e., size distribution and surface defects) involved in the excitation wavelength dependent emission in both DCPL and UCPL carbon dots.

In summary, we have synthesized highly luminescent CDs by a facile one-step pyrolysis process from 10-Undeceonic acid. The synthesized CDs exhibit emission energy in deep UV region, ∼ 3.5 eV. The CDs at different pyrolysis time intervals possess color-tunable and excitation wavelength-dependent PL properties, with emission wavelength covering the visible and near-infrared spectral region. Both typical and upconversion properties were observed in excitation wavelength ranging approximately 550 nm ≤ λex ≤ 694 nm. The present study provides an additional pathway towards new type of fluorescence and upconversion-based applications, including solar energy harvesting, color displays, and photocatalysis.

Naveen Kumar Reddy Bogireddy: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Vivechana Agarwal: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Resources, Software, Supervision, Project administration, Validation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: VA acknowledges the funds provided by CONACyT project A1-S-30393. NKRB acknowledges DGAPA- UNAM for postdoctoral fellowship.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Materials and methods section; UV-visible Absorbance; fluorescence studies; FTIR and size distribution from Dynamic Light Scattering presented in the supplementary information. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2021.131640.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
